meta.title: WebPPL Distributions — Interactive Guide
meta.description: Build intuition for probability distributions in WebPPL through interactive examples
meta.author: Clawd

children.children: Probability distributions are the building blocks of probabilistic programs. In WebPPL, distributions are first-class objects you can sample from, score, and compose. This tutorial builds intuition through interactive examples.
**Core idea:** A distribution represents uncertainty. `sample(dist)` draws a random value. `dist.score(val)` tells you how (un)surprising that value is.

props.title: 🎲 Part 1: Discrete Distributions (Coin Flips, Dice, Categories)

children.children: Discrete distributions have countable outcomes (true/false, integers, categories). Let's start with the simplest: **Bernoulli** (a biased coin).

props.title: Bernoulli: The Biased Coin

children.children: **Try this:**
Run it multiple times — you'll get `true` about 70% of the time
Change `p: 0.7` to `p: 0.1` — now `true` is rare
Try `coin.score(true)` — this is `log(0.7) ≈ -0.36`. Low surprise = high probability.
Try `coin.score(false)` — this is `log(0.3) ≈ -1.2`. Higher surprise = lower probability.
`.score(val)` returns the **log-probability** of `val`. Why log?
**Numerical stability:** Probabilities can be tiny (0.0001). Logs avoid underflow.
**Composability:** `log(p1 * p2) = log(p1) + log(p2)`. Products become sums.
**Information theory:** `-log(p)` is "surprisal" (Shannon information). Rare events are surprising.
**Rule of thumb:** More negative score = less probable. `score(val) = 0` means `P(val) = 1` (certainty).

props.title: Categorical: Weighted Die

children.children: **Experiments:**
Fair die: `ps: [1,1,1,1,1,1]` — each face equally likely
Loaded die: `ps: [10,1,1,1,1,1]` — ⚀ is 10× more likely
Check: `die.score('⚀')` vs `die.score('⚁')` — scores reflect the weights
**Key insight:** `ps` don't need to sum to 1. WebPPL normalizes them. `ps: [2,2,2]` = `ps: [1,1,1]`.

Imagine each element in `vs` is a candidate. `ps` are their vote counts. `sample()` picks a candidate with probability proportional to their votes. `ps: [100, 50, 30]` means the first candidate gets ~55.5% of the total votes.

props.title: Binomial: Flipping N Coins

children.children: Binomial answers: *"If I flip `n` fair coins (each with success probability `p`), how many heads?"*
`n: 10, p: 0.5` — fair coin, expect ~5 heads
`n: 100, p: 0.01` — 100 trials, 1% success rate → expect ~1 success
Score the expected value: `experiment.score(5)` (for n=10, p=0.5) is high. `experiment.score(0)` is very negative (rare).
You show 1000 users a new button. Each clicks with probability `p` (unknown). You observe 47 clicks. What's `p`?
This is **Bayesian inference**: condition on observed data (47 clicks), infer the latent parameter (`p`).

props.title: 📈 Part 2: Continuous Distributions (Numbers on a Line)

children.children: Continuous distributions have uncountably many outcomes (any real number in a range). The workhorse is **Gaussian** (normal distribution).

props.title: Gaussian: The Bell Curve

children.children: **Intuition:**
`mu` (mean): center of the bell curve. Most samples near here.
`sigma` (std dev): spread. Small σ = tight cluster. Large σ = wide spread.
68% of samples within `mu ± sigma`, 95% within `mu ± 2*sigma`
`sigma: 1` — almost everyone between 169-171 cm
`sigma: 30` — huge variance, people from 110-230 cm
`height.score(170)` vs `height.score(200)` — which is more surprising?

**Central Limit Theorem:** Sum many independent random variables → result is Gaussian (even if the originals aren't). Example: height = genetics + nutrition + environment + ... Each factor adds a bit. The sum? Gaussian.
**Maximum entropy:** If you only know the mean and variance, Gaussian is the least-assuming distribution. It's the "I don't know" prior for continuous values.

props.title: Uniform: Any Value Equally Likely

children.children: Uniform(`a`, `b`) = flat line from `a` to `b`. Every value in `[a, b]` equally probable.
Use when: "I have no idea, could be anything in this range."
Score is constant: `random_time.score(10) == random_time.score(50)` (both in range)
Score is `-Infinity` outside range: `random_time.score(70)` (impossible)

props.title: Beta: Probability of a Probability

children.children: Beta is a distribution over `[0, 1]` — perfect for modeling probabilities. **Use case:** You have a coin, but you don't know if it's fair. Beta represents your uncertainty about its bias `p`.
`a` and `b` are pseudo-counts. Think: "I've seen `a-1` heads and `b-1` tails."
`Beta({a: 1, b: 1})` = Uniform (no data, all biases equally likely)
`Beta({a: 10, b: 2})` = bias toward high `p` (lots of heads observed)
`Beta({a: 100, b: 100})` = very confident `p ≈ 0.5` (equal heads/tails, large sample)

Start: `Beta({a: 1, b: 1})` — "I know nothing." Flip coin, get heads. Update: `Beta({a: 2, b: 1})` — "slightly biased toward heads." Flip again, get tails. Update: `Beta({a: 2, b: 2})` — "maybe fair?" Beta is your evolving belief about the coin's bias.
So far: distributions over single values (one number, one category). Now: distributions over **vectors** (multiple correlated values).

props.title: DiagCovGaussian: Independent Dimensions

children.children: This models a 2D vector: `[height, weight]`. Each dimension is independent Gaussian.
`mu: Vector([170, 70])` — mean height 170 cm, mean weight 70 kg
`sigma: Vector([10, 5])` — height varies ±10 cm, weight varies ±5 kg
**Key:** Diagonal covariance = dimensions are **uncorrelated**. Tall people aren't more/less likely to be heavy.

props.title: MultivariateGaussian: Correlated Dimensions

children.children: Now the covariance matrix has off-diagonal terms: `cov[0][1] = 40`. This means height and weight are **positively correlated**.
Positive cov: tall people tend to be heavier
Negative cov: one variable goes up, other goes down (e.g., altitude ↔ temperature)
Zero cov: independent (same as DiagCovGaussian)
**Visual:** DiagCovGaussian = circular/elliptical cloud aligned with axes. MultivariateGaussian = tilted ellipse.
**DiagCovGaussian:** Variables are independent. Faster, fewer parameters.
**MultivariateGaussian:** Variables correlate. More expressive, but O(d²) parameters for d dimensions.
**Rule of thumb:** Start with DiagCov. Upgrade to Multivariate if you need to model correlations.

props.title: Dirichlet: Distribution Over Probability Vectors

children.children: Dirichlet is like Beta, but for **probability vectors** (multiple categories that sum to 1).
`alpha: Vector([5, 2, 1])` — pseudo-counts for 3 categories
Higher alpha[i] → category `i` gets higher probability
Sum of alpha controls concentration: `[100, 100, 100]` = very confident equal split. `[1, 1, 1]` = uniform over simplex.
**Use case:** Topic modeling. Each document is a mix of topics. Dirichlet represents uncertainty about the mix.
WebPPL has distributions for specific use cases. Here are the essentials:

props.title: Delta: Deterministic "Distribution"

children.children: Delta assigns probability 1 to a single value. Not random at all! **Why use it?**
Uniform API: treat deterministic values as distributions
Marginal inference: `Infer()` returns a distribution. If the result is deterministic, it's a Delta.
**Warning:** Don't use Delta with random args in MCMC. It breaks the sampler.

props.title: Mixture: Combine Distributions

children.children: Mixture = weighted average of distributions. Here: 50% from first Gaussian (women's heights), 50% from second (men's heights).
Sample by: pick a component (via `Categorical(ps)`), then sample from it
Result: bimodal distribution (two peaks)
Change `ps: [0.9, 0.1]` → 90% women, 10% men

props.title: Poisson: Count Data (Rare Events)

children.children: Poisson models counts when events occur independently at a constant rate.
`mu`: average count (also the variance — Poisson has one parameter)
Use for: emails per hour, typos per page, customers per minute
Low `mu` = most samples near 0. High `mu` = approximates Gaussian.
Kernel Density Estimation: fit a distribution to observed data (non-parametric).
KDE smooths the data with Gaussian kernels. Result: continuous distribution that fits your observations. **Use when:** you have samples but don't know the underlying parametric form.

props.title: 🧠 Part 5: Connecting to Your Research (Language of Thought)

children.children: You're building a **three-layer framework**: neural kernels → symbolic operators → probabilistic programs. Distributions are the glue at **Layer 3**.
Schankian operators (ATRANS, PTRANS, etc.) aren't deterministic. They're **probabilistic state transformations**:
Now `ATRANS` is a **probabilistic program**. You can:
**Forward inference:** Given initial state, what are likely outcomes?
**Inverse inference (abduction):** Given final state, what operator sequence caused it?
**Learning:** Observe (before, event, after) triples, infer operator parameters (like success probability)
Symbolic regression finds operators. But operators have **parameters** (like ATRANS success rate). Use distributions to learn them:
This is **probabilistic program induction**: learn the parameters of discovered operators from data.

props.title: Distributions as Types in the Language of Thought

children.children: In your three-layer framework:
**Layer 1 (kernels):** Continuous transformations (Gaussian, Beta, etc.)
**Layer 2 (operators):** Discrete primitives (Categorical over operator types)
**Layer 3 (programs):** Compositions of operators, each with parameter distributions

**Key insight:** Distributions aren't just for modeling uncertainty. They're **types** in a probabilistic language. `Bernoulli` is the type of binary outcomes. `Categorical` is the type of discrete choices. `Gaussian` is the type of continuous magnitudes.

When you discover an operator via symbolic regression, you're discovering its **type signature** (inputs, outputs, parameter distributions). That signature IS the operator's definition in the language of thought.

**Thoreau's quote applies:** "As you simplify your life, the laws of the universe will be simpler." The right distributions (Beta for probabilities, Poisson for counts, Gaussian for sums) are the **simplest laws** that explain the data. Compression = finding the right distributional assumptions.

questions.question: You flip a coin 100 times, get 53 heads. Which distribution models the number of heads?
questions.explanation: Binomial models count of successes in n trials. Bernoulli is for a single trial. Gaussian is continuous (could work as approximation). Categorical is for different categories, not counts.

questions.question: Which distribution for modeling someone's height, given you know nothing but the range 150-200 cm?
questions.explanation: Uniform = 'no idea, equally likely anywhere in range'. Gaussian assumes concentration around a mean (you said you know nothing). Beta is for [0,1]. Exponential is for positive reals with decay.

questions.question: You want to model uncertainty about the bias of an unfair coin. Which distribution?
questions.explanation: Beta is the standard choice (conjugate prior for Bernoulli). Uniform({a:0, b:1}) is Beta({a:1, b:1}) — both represent 'no prior knowledge'. Bernoulli is the coin's distribution, not your uncertainty about it.

questions.question: What does `dist.score(val) = -5` mean?
options: The value is 5 standard deviations away
questions.explanation: Score is log-probability. exp(-5) ≈ 0.0067. Negative scores are normal (log of fraction < 1). Probabilities can't be negative.

questions.question: You observe samples [1.2, 1.5, 1.1, 8.3, 1.3]. Which distribution likely generated them?
questions.explanation: Most samples cluster around 1.2-1.5, but 8.3 is an outlier. Mixture of two Gaussians fits better than a single wide Gaussian. Uniform would produce values across the whole range evenly.

children.children: **Experiment:** Open [WebPPL online editor](http://webppl.org) and try the examples
**Read:** [Probabilistic Models of Cognition](http://probmods.org) (probmods.org) — full textbook using these distributions
**Build:** Implement a simple Bayesian model (e.g., coin bias inference from flips)
**Connect:** How do Schankian operators map to parameter distributions? (Your Layer 3 research)
